title: Custom AI ASICs Take Share — Broadcom + Arista Build the Ethernet AI Factory
summary: Hyperscaler dollars are shifting from merchant GPUs to custom silicon and Ethernet-based fabrics; Broadcom owns ~70% of the custom AI accelerator market with a $73B backlog, and Arista is the prime Ethernet beneficiary with $3.5B of AI fabric revenue locked for 2026.
catalyst: On May 27, 2026, Snowflake committed $6B to AWS over five years — its biggest cloud spend ever — anchored on AWS Graviton custom CPUs, the same day Marvell raised FY27 revenue guidance to ~$11B on accelerating custom-silicon design wins [1][2]. This caps a six-month sequence that began with the October 13, 2025 OpenAI–Broadcom announcement to co-develop 10 GW of OpenAI-designed XPUs deploying H2 2026 onward [3], followed by Broadcom shipping the 102.4 Tbps Tomahawk 6 Ethernet switch in production volume on March 12, 2026 [4].
thesis: Hyperscaler 2026 capex has been marked up to ~$725B (vs $388B in 2025) [5], and the incremental dollar is increasingly flowing to custom ASICs (forecast +44.6% YoY in 2026) rather than merchant GPUs (+16.1%) [6]. Broadcom is the indispensable design partner — Google TPU, Meta MTIA, OpenAI XPU, plus three additional confirmed customers — capturing the silicon margin pool, while Arista is the default Ethernet switching layer once a fabric scales beyond a single InfiniBand pod and is already deployed across OpenAI's Stargate Abilene and Microsoft Fairwater sites [7].
body_md:
## Setup

For two years the AI-infrastructure trade has been a NVIDIA monopsony narrative: hyperscalers buy GB200/GB300 systems at 70%+ gross margin, NVIDIA dictates terms, everyone else picks up scraps. The data accumulating through Q1 2026 earnings season — capped by today's Snowflake / AWS Graviton announcement and Marvell's beat-and-raise — shows that narrative has cracked. Hyperscalers are diverting an accelerating share of capex into **co-designed custom silicon** (XPUs) and the **Ethernet fabrics** that stitch those XPUs into million-accelerator clusters. The two clearest beneficiaries with reports in our universe are Broadcom (the silicon) and Arista Networks (the switches).

## Why Now

Three concrete data points from the last 75 days frame the inflection:

1. **OpenAI–Broadcom 10 GW deal** (announced October 13, 2025): OpenAI will co-design custom XPU accelerators with Broadcom and deploy 10 gigawatts of capacity starting H2 2026, with Broadcom supplying both the ASIC and the Ethernet networking stack [3]. Analyst estimates put this single program at up to $300B of cumulative AVGO revenue [8].
2. **Tomahawk 6 in production** (March 12, 2026): Broadcom began shipping the industry's first 102.4 Tbps Ethernet switch chip in production volume, doubling Tomahawk 5 throughput and targeting fabrics of more than one million XPUs [4]. Arista's flagship 7060X6 and 7800R4 platforms run on this silicon.
3. **Snowflake / AWS Graviton — $6B / 5 years** (May 27, 2026): Snowflake's AWS commitment quintupled from its 2020 IPO-era $1.2B, with the incremental compute running on AWS's custom Graviton and Trainium silicon — i.e., not NVIDIA [1]. Marvell reported the same evening, guiding FY27 revenue to ~$11B (up from a prior ~$10B view) on custom silicon design-win momentum [2].

Layered on top, Moody's and Futurum now estimate 2026 hyperscaler capex at $630–725B, up 62–77% over the $388B spent in 2025 [5]. Even a stable custom-silicon mix would lift AVGO and ANET revenue meaningfully; the mix is in fact widening.

## Company-Specific Edge

### Broadcom (AVGO) — the design-partner monopoly

Broadcom is the only merchant vendor offering an integrated stack of (a) custom ASIC design services on leading-edge TSMC nodes, (b) co-packaged optics, (c) Tomahawk / Jericho Ethernet switching, and (d) PCIe / NVLink-class interconnect IP. Sell-side now puts AVGO at **~70% market share** of the custom AI accelerator market [6], with Google TPU, Meta MTIA, and OpenAI XPU as anchor customers and three additional unnamed hyperscalers in development.

From our screener report (`reports/AVGO.json`): forward P/E **23.7×**, ROE **33.4%**, net margin **36.6%**, revenue growth YoY **29.5%**, PEG **0.94** — i.e., the company is priced at roughly a market multiple on forward earnings despite a $73B AI backlog and CEO Hock Tan's stated target of **$100B in AI chip revenue by FY2027** [9]. Q2 FY26 (reporting June 3, 2026) guidance is $22B total revenue, including **$10.7B AI revenue, +140% YoY** [9].

### Arista Networks (ANET) — the Ethernet exit ramp

Arista runs EOS on Broadcom Tomahawk silicon, and is now the dominant scale-out Ethernet vendor at hyperscalers building Ethernet AI fabrics. In Q1 2026 (reported May 5, 2026), revenue hit **$2.71B (+35.1% YoY)** and management raised full-year 2026 guidance to **$11.5B (27.7% growth)** with an explicit **$3.5B AI fabric revenue target — double 2025** [7][10]. Existing production deployments include OpenAI's Stargate Abilene supercomputer at Oracle Cloud, Microsoft's Fairwater facilities, and at least two unnamed Tier-1 cloud customers.

Screener metrics (`reports/ANET.json`): forward P/E **28.3×**, ROE **31.4%**, net margin **39.0%**, revenue growth YoY **28.9%**. The valuation premium versus AVGO is reasonable given ANET's pure-play AI-networking exposure and higher gross margin profile.

## Numbers That Matter

| Metric | AVGO | ANET |
|---|---|---|
| Forward P/E | 23.7× | 28.3× |
| ROE | 33.4% | 31.4% |
| Net margin | 36.6% | 39.0% |
| Revenue growth YoY (latest) | 29.5% | 35.1% |
| AI revenue (latest QTR guide / target) | $10.7B Q2 FY26 (+140% YoY) | $3.5B FY26 target |
| Backlog / visibility | $73B AI backlog through FY28 | TSMC capacity through 2028 secured by AVGO |
| IOWN AI Resilience | 10/10 | 9/10 |
| Overall IOWN score | 80 (BUY) | 80 (BUY) |

Key industry numbers: custom ASIC shipments forecast **+44.6% YoY in 2026** versus **+16.1% for merchant GPUs** [6]; hyperscaler 2026 capex **$630–725B** [5]; OpenAI–Broadcom program 10 GW deploying H2 2026+ [3].

## Bear Case

Four things could break this trade:

1. **NVIDIA pricing war.** If NVIDIA cuts merchant GPU pricing aggressively (Blackwell Ultra / Rubin transition), the savings from custom silicon shrink and ROI for in-house ASIC programs deteriorates. Watch NVDA gross margin guidance — a step-down below 70% would signal this.
2. **Hyperscaler digestion.** A capex pause from any of Microsoft / Meta / Amazon / Google would compress AVGO and ANET multiples regardless of unit economics. Current Q2 FY26 reporting cycle is the cleanest near-term test.
3. **InfiniBand defends scale-up.** Mellanox/NVDA InfiniBand still leads single-pod scale-up; if optical NVLink (rather than Ethernet) wins the rack-to-rack tier, ANET's TAM compresses.
4. **Geopolitical TSMC risk.** Both companies depend on TSMC leading-edge nodes (N3, N2). A Taiwan-strait shock — not currently in base case — would hit AVGO harder than ANET.

## Conclusion

This is not a 'NVIDIA short' thesis — NVIDIA continues to dominate merchant GPU demand and is itself growing AI revenue >50% YoY. The opportunity is that the **second derivative** of hyperscaler spending — the incremental dollar above 2025 levels — is increasingly flowing to (1) the custom ASIC partner with leverage at every hyperscaler (AVGO) and (2) the Ethernet vendor that scales those ASICs into million-XPU fabrics (ANET). The Snowflake / Graviton signal today and the Marvell guide-up tonight both reinforce the structural shift, with the AVGO Q2 FY26 print on June 3 as the next confirming catalyst.

ticker_rationale.AVGO: 70% market share of custom AI accelerators, $73B AI backlog, Q2 FY26 AI revenue guided to $10.7B (+140% YoY); priced at 23.7x forward P/E with PEG 0.94 — IOWN score 80, AI Resilience 10/10.
ticker_rationale.ANET: $3.5B AI fabric revenue target for 2026 (double 2025), FY26 guidance raised to $11.5B (+27.7%), already deployed at OpenAI Stargate Abilene and Microsoft Fairwater; 39% net margin, IOWN score 80, AI Resilience 9/10.

risks: NVIDIA cuts merchant GPU pricing on Blackwell Ultra / Rubin transition, eroding the cost savings that justify custom-silicon programs and slowing hyperscaler ASIC adoption
A hyperscaler capex pause (any of MSFT/META/AMZN/GOOG cuts 2026 capex guide by 10%+ on a quarterly print) compresses AVGO and ANET multiples even if backlog is unchanged
InfiniBand / NVLink optical defends the rack-to-rack scale-up tier, compressing Arista's Ethernet TAM to scale-out only
Taiwan-strait disruption: both companies depend on TSMC N3/N2 capacity; any TSMC export-control or geopolitical shock hits AVGO more than ANET
Customer concentration: OpenAI internalizes design IP after Phase 1 of the 10 GW program and renegotiates AVGO's take rate
invalidation: Broadcom Q2 FY26 (June 3, 2026) AI revenue prints below $9.5B, more than 10% below the $10.7B guide
Broadcom AI backlog declines sequentially from the reported $73B in any quarter through FY27
Arista cuts FY26 revenue guidance below $11.0B (from the May 2026 $11.5B raise), or AI fabric target below $3.0B (from $3.5B)
OpenAI announces a major XPU program cancellation or a multi-quarter delay of the 10 GW Broadcom buildout
AVGO stock breaks below $300 (vs $430 screener reference) on fundamental — not macro — news, indicating thesis erosion
